---
title: "Project Proposal"
format: html
---

# Milestone 1: Proposal + Dataset

## Title

**Exploring Equity in Metro Transit: A Spatial Analysis of Ridership, Access, and Income**

## Team Members

- Rishika Kundu
- Hadley Wilkins
- Laurice Jimu

## Project Description

This project explores Metro Transit spatial data alongside neighborhood income data to better understand how public transit serves different communities. We are interested in whether ridership, transit stop distribution, and service access vary across areas with different income levels. By combining transit and socioeconomic data, we hope to tell a clear and meaningful story about transportation equity in the Twin Cities.

## Broad Research Questions

We are interested in exploring the following questions:

1. Is there a relationship between neighborhood income levels and transit ridership?
2. Are transit stops and service lines distributed differently across higher-income and lower-income areas?
3. Which areas appear to be underserved and may need more transit lines, stops, or bus service?

## Reasons / Inspiration for Choosing This Topic

We chose this topic because we are interested in metro transit and in understanding how public services reach different communities. Transportation shapes access to work, school, and other opportunities, so it is important to examine whether transit systems serve all neighborhoods equitably. This project gives us the chance to combine spatial analysis, data wrangling, visualization, and storytelling around a real issue that affects daily life in the Twin Cities.

## Potential Dataset

One key dataset we plan to use is the Metro Transit count and headway spatial dataset from Minnesota Geospatial Commons:

- **Dataset:** Metro Transit Count and Headway Summary  
- **Source:** Minnesota Geospatial Commons  
- **Link:** <https://gisdata.mn.gov/dataset/us-mn-state-metc-trans-transit-count-headway-sum>

This dataset provides spatial information about transit service and ridership, which will allow us to examine where service is concentrated and how transit patterns relate to neighborhood characteristics. We also plan to pair this dataset with a second source containing area-level income data, such as U.S. Census or American Community Survey data.

## Communication Plan

Our primary communication channel will be a group text chat. We communicate there regularly and use it to coordinate tasks, ask questions, and share updates. We expect team members to respond within about 24 hours during the week whenever possible. We will also use class time as a standing meeting time to work on the project together, and we will continue conversations by text outside of class when we need help or need to make decisions.

## Team Roles

Although all team members will contribute to all major parts of the project, we identified lead roles to improve accountability and organization.

- **Point of Contact:** Laurice Jimu  
  Laurice will be the main person responsible for emailing the instructor and preceptors when needed.

- **Technical Lead:** Rishika Kundu  
  Rishika will oversee the code base, data workflow, and project website.

- **Style Lead:** Hadley Wilkins  
  Hadley will help ensure that the code follows the COMP/STAT 112/212 Code Styling Guide and remains readable and organized.

## Conflict Resolution Plan

If a team member misses a deadline, we will first talk as a team to understand the issue and decide how to adjust responsibilities so the project stays on track. We want to handle problems early through direct and respectful communication rather than letting them build up.

For project decisions, we will aim for discussion and consensus. Since there are three of us, ties are unlikely, but if we disagree, we will choose the option that best supports our research questions, project goals, and available data. We also plan to keep one another updated frequently so that misunderstandings do not turn into larger conflicts.

## Rough Implementation Plan

Our timeline is based on the course schedule and the project milestones listed in the course instructions. We used the remaining class schedule, including project workdays, to assign realistic dates.

### Milestone 1: Proposal + Dataset  
**Due: Wednesday, March 25, 2026**

Tasks:
- Finalize project topic and research questions  
- Confirm team roles and communication plan  
- Identify at least one main dataset  
- Draft and publish `proposal.qmd` on the project website  
- Create GitHub repository structure and begin GitHub Issues tracking  

Leads:
- Laurice: proposal writing and submission coordination  
- Rishika: repository and dataset setup  
- Hadley: editing and style review  

### Milestone 2: Effective Teamwork  
**Target completion: Monday, April 6, 2026**

Tasks:
- Each team member will read, watch, or listen to one resource about effective teamwork
- Each team member will complete their own summary in the team progress tracker

Leads:
- Individual responsibility for each team member

### Milestone 3: Case Study  
**Target completion: Wednesday, April 8, 2026**

Tasks:
- Select one professional data story from the examples provided in the course materials
- Analyze how the story is structured, including motivation, data use, visual flow, and communication style
- Add the case study write-up to `appx/case-study.qmd`
- Include citations for referenced materials

Leads:
- Hadley: case study organization and writing lead  
- All: contribute observations and examples  

### Milestone 4: Individual EDA  
**Target completion: Friday, April 10, 2026**

Tasks:
- Each team member completes an individual exploratory data analysis on one project dataset
- Save work in their respective Quarto files under the `eda` folder
- Explore distributions, missingness, geographic variation, and key patterns
- Generate early visualizations and notes on possible findings

Leads:
- Rishika: transit dataset EDA  
- Hadley: neighborhood or socioeconomic dataset EDA  
- Laurice: spatial pattern exploration and visualization ideas  

### Milestone 5: Progress Presentation  
**Due: Wednesday, April 22, 2026**

Tasks:
- Combine early findings into a clear project update
- Summarize research questions, data sources, cleaning decisions, and preliminary visualizations
- Prepare and deliver the project progress presentation in class
- Collect feedback for revision

Leads:
- Laurice: presentation flow and speaking coordination  
- Rishika: technical explanation of data and methods  
- Hadley: slide clarity, formatting, and polish  

### Milestone 6: Initial Presentation / Near-Final Story  
**Target completion: Friday, May 1, 2026**

Tasks:
- Finalize the main data story structure for the website
- Refine visualizations for clarity and effectiveness
- Connect results directly to research questions
- Prepare near-final presentation and incorporate class feedback

Leads:
- All team members contribute to analysis, presentation, and reporting
- Rishika: final technical integration  
- Laurice: narrative flow and interpretation  
- Hadley: readability and styling  

### Milestone 7: Final Deliverables + Evaluation  
**Final edits due: Monday, May 4, 2026**

Tasks:
- Finalize `index.qmd` data story report
- Make sure the website is published and links work properly
- Hide code, warnings, and unnecessary output in the final report
- Finalize `README.md`
- Embed presentation slides and presentation video
- Check that all files are stored in the repository with clean file organization
- Submit final deliverables
- Complete required evaluations and reflections

Leads:
- Laurice: final proofreading and deliverable checks  
- Rishika: website publishing, code integration, and reproducibility  
- Hadley: style consistency, code readability, and final editing  

## GitHub Issues Plan

We will use GitHub Issues and Milestones to track project progress throughout the semester. We plan to create GitHub milestones that match the major project phases above, and then create smaller issues under each milestone with assigned team members and due dates.

Planned GitHub milestones:

1. Proposal + Dataset  
2. Effective Teamwork  
3. Case Study  
4. EDA  
5. Progress Presentation  
6. Initial Presentation  
7. Final Deliverables  

Examples of issues we expect to create:
- Draft proposal.qmd
- Set up repository folders and website
- Download and inspect transit dataset
- Identify second dataset for income measures
- Complete individual EDA file
- Write case study appendix
- Build progress presentation slides
- Draft final report sections
- Embed slides and video into website
- Final review of README and published site

## Closing Note

Our team is excited to use this project to study how transit access and ridership relate to income and geography in the Twin Cities. We hope to build a project that is technically strong, visually effective, and accessible to a broad audience. Because this course emphasizes both data science skills and communication, we want our final product to tell a clear story about transit equity while demonstrating careful data wrangling, spatial analysis, and thoughtful visualization.
